structured_choices.data_source: EHR records spanning 36 months (T-36 to T0) from a single healthcare provider system, comprising 100,000 patient records with medication adherence time series and 30-day readmission labels. Data includes: (1) medication adherence logs with timestamps and adherence rates (proportion of doses taken on schedule); (2) discharge dates and 30-day readmission outcomes; (3) baseline demographics (age, sex, insurance type); (4) provider identifiers and discharge department; (5) comorbidity scores (Charlson index) and discharge summary data available at discharge time.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: readmission within 30 calendar days of discharge. Label is assigned at T0 (30 days post-discharge) and is considered observed and fixed. Patients without 30-day follow-up records in the EHR are excluded from the analysis to avoid censoring bias.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split stratified by discharge department. The cohort is divided chronologically by discharge date: months 1–24 for training (60%), months 25–30 for validation (20%), months 31–36 for test (20%). Within each temporal fold, patients are stratified by discharge department (e.g., cardiology, orthopedics, general medicine) to ensure each department is represented proportionally in train/val/test, addressing confounding due to variability in provider practices. This approach prevents future information leakage (adherence data cannot precede the discharge date) and accounts for secular trends or system-level changes over the three-year period.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing and feature engineering transformers are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–24). Specifically: (1) adherence time series are normalized using z-score scaling parameters computed on training data; (2) missing adherence values are imputed using forward-fill then mean imputation, with imputation parameters (mean, fill order) derived from training data; (3) any categorical encoding (e.g., department one-hot encoding) is defined from training data. These fitted transformers are then applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Baseline demographic features (age, Charlson score) are also standardized using training-set statistics.
structured_choices.features: Two feature sets are constructed: (RNN input) the full medication adherence time series for the 180 days preceding discharge, represented as a sequence of adherence rates (daily or aggregated to weekly bins), padded or truncated to uniform length (180 timesteps). (Logistic regression input) baseline demographics: age, sex, insurance type, Charlson comorbidity score, discharge department, and the mean and standard deviation of adherence over the entire pre-discharge period (to give LR a 'summary' of adherence behavior). No future information (e.g., readmission outcome, post-discharge events) is included in either feature set. All features are derived from data available at the time of discharge.
structured_choices.model: RNN model: a bidirectional LSTM with 64 hidden units, one layer, dropout (0.3), trained end-to-end with binary cross-entropy loss and Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-3). The RNN ingests the 180-timestep adherence sequence and outputs a single prediction (probability of 30-day readmission). Hyperparameters (hidden units, dropout, learning rate) are tuned on the validation set.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression model trained on the same baseline demographic features (age, sex, insurance, Charlson score, department, mean/SD adherence). The LR model is given equal tuning effort: regularization strength (L2 penalty) is selected via grid search on the validation set using the same metric (see below). Both models use the same training data (months 1–24) and are evaluated on identical val/test splits and metrics. The baseline represents the claim that a simple, non-temporal model suffices; the RNN is superior if it yields meaningfully better performance by leveraging the sequential structure of adherence.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC). Justification: readmission is a low-prevalence outcome (class imbalance expected), and the clinical goal is to identify at-risk patients for intervention. AUPRC is insensitive to true negatives and reflects the trade-off between precision (reducing false alarms that waste intervention resources) and recall (avoiding missed at-risk patients). This aligns with the operational objective better than AUROC, which conflates performance on the majority class. The provider will use the model to flag high-risk patients; AUPRC reflects how well the model ranks true positives among predicted positives at various thresholds.
secondary_metrics: AUROC — to document performance across the full threshold spectrum and enable comparison to published benchmarks
F1 score at the validation-set optimal threshold — to balance precision and recall at a single operating point
Sensitivity (recall) at 90% specificity — to characterize the model's ability to catch true positives while maintaining a low false-positive rate
Calibration (expected calibration error and calibration plots) — to ensure predicted probabilities are well-calibrated, important for risk stratification in clinical settings
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Validation set (months 25–30) is used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. AUPRC computed on the validation set is the selection criterion for both RNN and logistic regression. The test set (months 31–36) is held out and evaluated only once, after model selection is complete, to obtain an unbiased estimate of generalization performance.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is not accessed during any aspect of model development, hyperparameter tuning, or feature engineering. After both the RNN and logistic regression models are finalized (based on validation performance), the test set is used once to compute the primary metric (AUPRC) and all secondary metrics for both models. Test-set results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap).
structured_choices.confound_controls: Provider practice variability: stratified split ensures each department is represented in train/val/test proportionally, and department is included as a feature in both models. Residual provider-level confounding is assessed post-hoc via stratified analysis (compute AUPRC separately for each department on the test set to detect if model performance varies systematically by provider). Patient socioeconomic factors (social determinants of health): while not directly available in EHR, insurance type is included as a proxy and is adjusted for in both models. Temporal trends: the temporal split naturally accounts for any system-level drift over the 36-month period. Class imbalance: both models use the same training data; imbalance is not addressed via resampling (to maintain realistic class distributions), but is accounted for in metric choice (AUPRC) and secondary metrics (F1, sensitivity). Post-hoc analysis examines performance across readmission risk strata (low, medium, high baseline risk by Charlson score) to detect if either model exhibits bias.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy
The experiment uses 36 months of EHR records from a single healthcare provider system, comprising approximately 100,000 patient records. The data includes timestamped medication adherence logs (daily or weekly aggregates), discharge dates, 30-day readmission outcomes, and baseline patient attributes (age, sex, insurance, comorbidity scores, discharge department). The outcome label is binary: readmission within 30 days of discharge. Patients lacking 30-day follow-up data are excluded to avoid censoring. All features and labels are collected prospectively and are available at the time of discharge or shortly thereafter; no retrospective imputation of missing outcomes is performed.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split Strategy
To prevent data leakage and account for provider-level confounding, the cohort is divided chronologically and departmentally. The timeline is split as follows: months 1–24 (training, 60%), months 25–30 (validation, 20%), months 31–36 (test, 20%). Within each temporal fold, patients are stratified by discharge department (e.g., cardiology, orthopedics, general medicine) to ensure proportional representation. This temporal split ensures that adherence data precedes discharge and readmission outcomes, eliminating future information leakage. Departmental stratification addresses variability in provider practices and readmission protocols across clinical units.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Two feature sets are constructed, one for each model. For the RNN, the primary input is the medication adherence time series for the 180 days preceding discharge, represented as a sequence of adherence rates (e.g., weekly averages of the proportion of doses taken on schedule). The sequence is padded or truncated to a uniform length of 180 timesteps. For logistic regression, features include: baseline demographics (age, sex, insurance type), Charlson comorbidity score, discharge department, and summary statistics of adherence (mean and standard deviation over the pre-discharge period). All preprocessing—z-score normalization of adherence, forward-fill then mean imputation of missing values, and categorical encoding—is fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents any information from validation or test leaking into the preprocessing parameters.

### 4. Model and Baseline
The RNN model is a bidirectional LSTM with 64 hidden units, one recurrent layer, dropout (0.3), and a sigmoid output layer producing a probability of 30-day readmission. It is trained with binary cross-entropy loss and Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-3). The logistic regression baseline uses the same training data and baseline feature set (demographics and adherence summary statistics), allowing a fair comparison: both models are trained on the same 60% of patients (months 1–24) and given equivalent tuning effort (hyperparameter search on validation data). The baseline tests the null hypothesis that a simple, non-temporal model is sufficient; the RNN is superior only if it materially improves performance by exploiting the sequential structure of adherence.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
The primary metric is area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC). Readmission is a low-prevalence outcome, and the clinical objective is to identify at-risk patients for timely intervention. AUPRC directly reflects the trade-off between precision (reducing false alarms) and recall (minimizing missed cases) and is insensitive to the majority class, making it more appropriate than AUROC for imbalanced settings. Secondary metrics include AUROC (for threshold-independent comparison), F1 score at the optimal validation threshold, sensitivity at 90% specificity (to characterize false-positive rate at a fixed operating point), and calibration metrics (expected calibration error, calibration plots) to ensure predicted probabilities are clinically actionable.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection
Hyperparameters for both the RNN (hidden unit count, dropout, learning rate) and logistic regression (L2 regularization) are selected via grid or random search, evaluated on the validation set (months 25–30) using AUPRC as the selection criterion. The test set (months 31–36) is held in reserve and is not accessed until after model selection is finalized. Final performance estimates, including 95% confidence intervals, are computed on the test set using all evaluation metrics.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope
Provider variability is controlled via departmental stratification and by including department as a feature in both models. Post-hoc stratified analysis on the test set (separate AUPRC by department) will detect any systematic performance variation by provider. Temporal trends are naturally accounted for by the chronological split. Patient socioeconomic factors are partially controlled via insurance type as a proxy. Class imbalance is not corrected via resampling (to maintain realistic prevalence) but is addressed through metric choice (AUPRC) and secondary metrics. The experiment establishes whether the RNN outperforms logistic regression on this specific provider's data over the 36-month window; generalization to other providers, healthcare systems, or populations is out of scope and would require external validation.
